criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the FCI score definition and how attention-based branching is motivated by massive attention values in LLMs.
The agent has read Section 3.1.1 and understands that responses are segmented into steps using double newlines ('\n\n'), yielding T_k steps per response.

The agent has read Equation 5 and understands that the FCI score for step k at layer l and head h is computed by summing attention weights of all subsequent steps j from k+Δ to T_k attending to step k.
The agent has read Equation 6 and understands that the final FCI score y_k is obtained by taking the maximum across all layers and heads.
The agent has read Section 3.1.3 and understands the ATB rule: select steps in the top 20% by FCI score, then pick the top N=2 earliest steps as branching points.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the two components of adaptive sampling: attention-based filtering and difficulty-aware expansion.
The agent has read Equation 8 and understands that problems whose average FCI scores across initial responses are below the dataset mean are filtered out from Monte Carlo sampling.
The agent has read Equation 9 and understands that the number of trees M per problem is M = Round(exp(-z_n) × M'), where z_n is the difficulty score (fraction of correct initial samples) and M'=6.

The agent has read Section 3.2.2 and Equation 10 to understand that the prompt batch size is updated as B_{m+1} = Round(λB_m + (1-λ)(B'/B''_m)B_m) with λ=0.9, and responses with zero advantage are discarded after MC sampling.

The agent has read Section 3.3 and understands that in one-step off-policy training, initial sampling for the (m+1)-th batch is performed concurrently with MC sampling for the m-th batch, eliminating redundant sampling.

The agent has read Section 4.1 and noted all key hyperparameters: training batch size 64, PPO minibatch size 32, learning rate 1e-6, Clip-Higher ε_high=0.28, KL loss weight 0.001, λ=0.9, ρ=0.2, Δ=4, N=2, M'=6.

The agent has read Section 4.1 and identified the evaluation benchmarks (AIME24, AIME25, AMC23, MATH-500, Minerva Math, OlympiadBench), metrics (Pass@1 and Pass@K with K=32 for AIME/AMC23 and K=4 for others), and the evaluation prompt template from Appendix A.
The plan includes step-level segmentation of model responses using double newline ('\n\n') as a delimiter to produce a sequence of reasoning steps.
The plan includes extracting token-level attention scores via a forward pass and aggregating them into step-to-step attention matrices α^{l,h} ∈ R^{T_k × T_k}.
The plan includes computing per-step FCI scores using Equations 5 and 6, with Δ=4 and max aggregation over layers and heads.
The plan includes implementing ATB: identifying the top 20% steps by FCI score and selecting the top 2 earliest among them as branching points (Equation 7).
The plan includes both components of adaptive sampling: attention-based filtering using mean FCI threshold (Equation 8) and difficulty-aware expansion using exponential scaling (Equation 9).
The plan includes adaptive batch size update (Equation 10) and discarding zero-advantage samples to ensure valid training batches.
The plan includes the one-step off-policy training pipeline where initial sampling for batch m+1 is overlapped with MC sampling for batch m.

The plan specifies using DS-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B and DS-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B as base models, training on DeepScaleR-Preview-Dataset (40.3k problems), and using verl as the training framework with vLLM for rollout generation.

The plan details running main experiments against GRPO, TreeRL, and DeepScaleR-Preview-1.5B on six mathematical benchmarks, and running an ablation study with the five configurations listed in Table 2.
The code correctly segments model responses into steps by splitting on double newlines ('\n\n'), and handles edge cases such as empty steps or single-step responses.

The code performs a single forward pass through the model to extract token-level attention weights from all layers and heads and aggregates them into step-to-step attention matrices α^{l,h} ∈ R^{T_k × T_k} by summing token-level weights within each step boundary.
The code implements Equation 5: for each step k, layer l, and head h, the FCI score y_k^{l,h} is computed as the sum of α_{j,k}^{l,h} for all j from k+Δ to T_k, with Δ=4.
The code implements Equation 6: the final FCI score y_k is computed as the maximum over all layers l and heads h of y_k^{l,h}.

The code implements Equation 7: the candidate branching set C contains all steps k whose FCI score meets or exceeds the 80th percentile (ρ=0.2 quantile level), and then the top N=2 earliest steps from C are selected as final branching points.

The code implements TreeRL's tree-based advantage computation: node value V(s_k) = average accuracy of all leaf children (Equation 2), and advantage Â_{i,k} = (1/√|L(s_k)|) × (global_advantage + local_advantage) (Equation 3).

The code implements Equation 8: attention-based filtering that computes the average FCI score per problem across initial responses and filters out problems whose mean FCI score is below the dataset-wide mean, keeping only problems in D_MC.

The code implements Equation 9: difficulty-aware expansion that computes the difficulty score z_n = (1/G) Σ_i 1(o_i is correct) for each problem and sets the number of trees M = Round(exp(-z_n) × M') with M'=6.

The code implements Equation 10: adaptive batch size update B_{m+1} = Round(λ × B_m + (1-λ) × (B'/B''_m) × B_m) with λ=0.9, where B' is the target batch size, B''_m is the valid training batch size (non-zero advantage), and B_m is the current prompt batch size.
The code correctly discards all samples with zero advantage values after MC sampling before constructing the final training batch.
The code implements the one-step off-policy training pipeline: at step m, initial sampling for the (m+1)-th batch is performed simultaneously with MC sampling for the m-th batch.
The code uses the GRPO policy optimization loss (Equation 1) with token-level policy loss, Clip-Higher with ε_high=0.28, and KL divergence penalty with weight 0.001 for training.

The code correctly loads DS-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B and DS-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B as base models and integrates with the verl training framework and vLLM rollout backend with max response length 8192, top-p 1.0, and temperature 1.0.

The code loads and preprocesses the DeepScaleR-Preview-Dataset of 40.3k mathematical reasoning problems and applies the evaluation prompt template '{problem} Let\'s think step by step and output the final answer within \\boxed{}.'

The code implements the three PSRL baselines for comparison: (1) GRPO (outcome-based, no critic, group-relative advantage), (2) TreeRL (entropy-based tree branching), and (3) DeepScaleR-Preview-1.5B (strong RL-trained reference model).
The code implements the hybrid answer verifier combining DeepScaleR's verifier and Math-Verify to check correctness of model outputs on all six benchmarks.

The five ablation variants are correctly implemented: TreeRL baseline, TreeRL w/first-2-step branching, TreeRL w/ATB only, w/ATB+ADS without attention-based filtering, and w/ATB+ADS without difficulty-aware expansion.

The main training experiments for AttnRL are executed successfully on DS-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B for 500 training steps using the DeepScaleR-Preview-Dataset with training batch size 64 and PPO minibatch size 32.
The main training experiments for AttnRL are executed successfully on DS-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B using three nodes with 8× NVIDIA H800 GPUs each.
GRPO and TreeRL baseline training experiments are executed successfully on both DS-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B and DS-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B for fair comparison.
All six ablation study variants are executed successfully on DS-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B and results are collected across all six benchmarks.

Evaluation is run on all six benchmarks (AIME24, AIME25, AMC23, MATH-500, Minerva Math, OlympiadBench) with the correct Pass@K values (K=32 for AIME24/AIME25/AMC23, K=4 for MATH-500/Minerva/OlympiadBench) and max response length 32768 tokens.

Training efficiency metrics are collected: wall-clock training time, number of valid training tokens (tokens with non-zero advantage), and final average performance for GRPO, TreeRL, TreeRL w/one-step off-policy, and AttnRL (matching Table 3).
The AttnRL results on DS-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B match Table 1: AIME24≈39.7%, AIME25≈28.5%, AMC23≈83.2%, MATH-500≈90.0%, Minerva≈40.3%, OlympiadBench≈61.4%, average≈57.2%.
The AttnRL results on DS-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B match Table 1: AIME24≈59.3%, AIME25≈42.5%, AMC23≈92.5%, MATH-500≈95.4%, Minerva≈49.3%, OlympiadBench≈73.3%, average≈68.7%.
AttnRL outperforms GRPO and TreeRL by approximately 1.9% and 1.8% average across six benchmarks at 1.5B scale, matching the reported improvements in Section 4.2.

The ablation results match Table 2: ATB alone improves over TreeRL by approximately 1.2% average; full AttnRL (ATB + ADS) achieves the highest average of 57.2%, confirming the contribution of each component.

Training efficiency results match Table 3: one-step off-policy reduces TreeRL wall-clock time by approximately 8% (from 67.7 to 62.2 hours), and AttnRL achieves 930.4M valid training tokens versus 274.6M for TreeRL.

The sampling analysis results (Figure 6 and Figure 7) qualitatively match: ATB reduces solve-all and solve-none ratios compared to entropy-based branching, and adaptive sampling reduces the fraction of zero-advantage samples while maintaining a stable valid training batch.

The preliminary disruption experiment results (Figure 3) are reproducible: disrupting top-20% FCI steps leads to a larger normalized accuracy drop (≈0.24) compared to disrupting non-top-20% steps (≈0.37), and entropy-based disruption falls between (≈0.30) on AIME24 using DS-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B.
